analogy: Think of "Photosynthesis in Higher Plants" through the chapter's own opening images: 166 BIOLOGY You have already studied the organisation of a flowering plant in Chapter 5. Have you ever thought about where and how the structures like roots, stems, leaves, flowers, fruits and seeds arise and that too in an orderly sequence? You are, by now,... The easy idea is to follow the chapter's own clues one by one instead of memorising a loose definition. First notice the words and examples the text repeats: 166 BIOLOGY, PLANT GROWTH AND DEVELOPMENT, CHAPTER 13, 13.1 Growth. Then connect each example to the question the chapter is trying to answer. Like solving a source-backed puzzle, every useful point must come from the chapter text itself and stay traceable to the NCERT source.
full_notes:
Photosynthesis in Higher Plants is decoded here only from the NCERT chapter text. The chapter begins with its own framing, examples and questions, then builds the topic through source-backed facts. The notes below keep that order: first the opening problem, then the important terms, then the mechanisms, examples and exam-useful connections that appear in the chapter itself.

The text explains that 166 BIOLOGY You have already studied the organisation of a flowering plant in Chapter 5.

The text explains that have you ever thought about where and how the structures like roots, stems, leaves, flowers, fruits and seeds arise and that too in an orderly sequence?

The text explains that you are, by now, aware of the terms seed, seedling, plantlet, mature plant.

The text explains that you have also seen that trees continue to increase in height or girth over a period of time.

The text explains that however, the leaves, flowers and fruits of the same tree not only have limited dimensions but also appear and fall periodically and some time repeatedly.

The text explains that why does vegetative phase precede flowering in a plant?

The text explains that all plant organs are made up of a variety of tissues; is there any relationship between the structure of a cell, a tissue, an organ and the function they perform?

The text explains that can the structure and the function of these be altered?

The text explains that all cells of a plant are descendents of the zygote.

The text explains that the question is, then, why and how do they have different structural and functional attributes?

The text explains that development is the sum of two processes: growth and differentiation.

The text explains that to begin with, it is essential and sufficient to know that the development of a mature plant from a zygote (fertilised egg) follow a precise and highly ordered succession of events.

The text explains that during this process a complex body organisation is formed that produces roots, leaves, branches, flowers, fruits, and seeds, and eventually they die (Figure 13.1).

The text explains that the first step in the process of plant growth is seed germination.

The text explains that the seed germinates when favourable conditions for growth exist in the environment.

The text explains that in absence of such favourable conditions the seeds do not germinate and goes into a period of suspended growth or rest.

The text explains that in this chapter, you shall also study some of the factors which govern and control these developmental processes.

The text explains that pLANT GROWTH AND DEVELOPMENT CHAPTER 13 13.1 Growth 13.2 Differentiation, Dedifferentiation and Redifferentiation 13.3 Development 13.4 Plant Growth Regulators

The text explains that generally, growth is accompanied by metabolic processes (both anabolic and catabolic), that occur at the expense of energy.

The text explains that since increase in protoplasm is difficult to measure directly, one generally measures some quantity which is more or less proportional to it.

The text explains that arrows exhibit the direction of growth of cells and organ G F E D C B A Figure 13.3 Detection of zones of elongation by the parallel line technique.

The text explains that in most systems, the initial growth is slow (lag phase), and it increases rapidly thereafter – at an exponential rate (log or exponential phase).

The text explains that pLANT GROWTH AND DEVELOPMENT 171 Quantitative comparisons between the growth of living system can also be made in two ways : (i) measurement and the comparison of total growth per unit time is called the absolute growth rate.

The text explains that (ii) The growth of the given system per unit time expressed on a common basis, e.g., per unit initial parameter is called the relative growth rate.

The text explains that nutrients (macro and micro essential elements) are required by plants for the synthesis of protoplasm and act as source of energy.

The text explains that in addition, every plant organism has an optimum temperature range best suited for its growth.

The text explains that try to correlate the various anatomical features you encounter in plants to the functions they perform.

The text explains that now, we may say that even differentiation in plants is open, because cells/tissues arising out of the same meristem have different structures at maturity.

The text explains that the final structure at maturity of a cell/tissue is also determined by the location of the cell within.

The text explains that diagrammatic representation of the sequence of processes which constitute the development of a cell of a higher plant is given in Figure 13.8.

The text explains that pLANT GROWTH AND DEVELOPMENT 173 Plants follow different pathways in response to environment or phases of life to form different kinds of structures.

The text explains that figure 13.8 Sequence of the developmental process in a plant cell Cell Division Death Plasmatic growth Differentiation Expansion (Elongation) Maturation MERISTEMATIC CELL SENESCENCE MATURE CELL Figure 13.9 Heterophylly in (a) larkspur and (b) buttercup

The text explains that the former includes both intracellular (genetic) or intercellular factors (chemicals such as plant growth regulators) while the latter includes light, temperature, water, oxygen, nutrition, etc.

The text explains that all this started with the observation of Charles Darwin and his son Francis Darwin when they observed that the coleoptiles of canary grass responded to unilateral illumination by growing towards the light source (phototropism).

The text explains that figure 13.10 Experiment used to demonstrate that tip of the coleoptile is the source of auxin.

The text explains that gA3 is used to speed up the malting process in brewing industry.

The text explains that since ethylene regulates so many physiological processes, it is one of the most widely used PGR in agriculture.

The text explains that the most widely used compound as source of ethylene is ethephon.

The text explains that many of the extrinsic factors such as temperature and light, control plant growth and development via PGR.

The text explains that we shall discuss briefly the role of light and temperature (both of them, the extrinsic factors) on initiation of flowering.

The text explains that differentiation results in development of structures that is commensurate with the function the cells finally has to perform.

The text explains that plant growth and development is also affected by light, temperature, nutrition, oxygen status, gravity and such external factors.

The text explains that why is not any one parameter good enough to demonstrate growth throughout the life of a flowering plant?

The text explains that describe briefly: (a) Arithmetic growth (b) Geometric growth (c) Sigmoid growth curve (d) Absolute and relative growth rates 4.

The text explains that list five main groups of natural plant growth regulators.

The text explains that write a note on discovery, physiological functions and agricultural/horticultural applications of any one of them.

The text explains that why is abscisic acid also known as stress hormone?

The text explains that 6. ‘Both growth and differentiation in higher plants are open’.

The text explains that 7. ‘Both a short day plant and a long day plant can produce can flower simultaneously in a given place’.

The text explains that which one of the plant growth regulators would you use if you are asked to: (a) induce rooting in a twig (b) quickly ripen a fruit (c) delay leaf senescence (d) induce growth in axillary buds (e) ‘bolt’ a rosette plant (f) induce immediate stomatal closure in leaves.

The text explains that would a defoliated plant respond to photoperiodic cycle?

The text explains that what would be expected to happen if: (a) GA3 is applied to rice seedlings (b) dividing cells stop differentiating (c) a rotten fruit gets mixed with unripe fruits (d) you forget to add cytokinin to the culture medium.

Source: NCERT Class 11 Biology, Chapter 13: Photosynthesis in Higher Plants

cases.name: PLANT GROWTH AND DEVELOPMENT CHAPTER
cases.point: PLANT GROWTH AND DEVELOPMENT CHAPTER 13 13.1 Growth 13.2 Differentiation, Dedifferentiation and Redifferentiation 13.3 Development 13.4 Plant Growth Regulators (NCERT Class 11 Biology Ch13 pg 1)

cases.name: Plant Growth Generally
cases.point: 13.1.1 Plant Growth Generally is Indeterminate Plant growth is unique because plants retain the capacity for unlimited growth throughout their life. (NCERT Class 11 Biology Ch13 pg 1)

cases.name: You
cases.point: You know that they are responsible for Seed coat Epicotyl hook Cotyledons Cotyledon Soil line Epicotyl Hypocotyl Hypocotyl Figure 13.1 Germination and seedling development in bean (NCERT Class 11 Biology Ch13 pg 1)

cases.name: Growth
cases.point: 13.1.2 Growth is Measurable Growth, at a cellular level, is principally a consequence of increase in the amount of protoplasm. (NCERT Class 11 Biology Ch13 pg 2)

cases.name: Phases
cases.point: 13.1.3 Phases of Growth The period of growth is generally divided into three phases, namely, meristematic, elongation and maturation (Figure 13.3). (NCERT Class 11 Biology Ch13 pg 2)

schemes.name: BIOLOGY You
schemes.point: 166 BIOLOGY You have already studied the organisation of a flowering plant in Chapter 5. (NCERT Class 11 Biology Ch13 pg 1)

schemes.name: During
schemes.point: During this process a complex body organisation is formed that produces roots, leaves, branches, flowers, fruits, and seeds, and eventually they die (Figure 13.1). (NCERT Class 11 Biology Ch13 pg 1)

schemes.name: Nutrients
schemes.point: Nutrients (macro and micro essential elements) are required by plants for the synthesis of protoplasm and act as source of energy. (NCERT Class 11 Biology Ch13 pg 5)

schemes.name: This
schemes.point: This act leading to maturation is termed as differentiation. (NCERT Class 11 Biology Ch13 pg 6)

schemes.name: PGRs
schemes.point: PGRs may act synergistically or antagonistically. (NCERT Class 11 Biology Ch13 pg 13)

prelims_traps: Do not treat 166 BIOLOGY and PLANT GROWTH AND DEVELOPMENT as identical unless the source text links them directly.
Do not add outside examples to Photosynthesis in Higher Plants; use only the examples and processes named in the chapter.
Do not confuse a visible surface result with the internal process or source evidence described by the text.
mains_angles: Explain Photosynthesis in Higher Plants through the source chain: evidence, process and visible result.
Use 166 BIOLOGY and PLANT GROWTH AND DEVELOPMENT to show how the chapter connects facts with mechanisms.
Frame an answer around causes, internal working, surface expression and source limitations from the NCERT chapter.
